perturbation.original_text: (vii) Receipt or acceptance by Lender (or its authorized representative) of a royalty statement or receipt or acceptance of any accompanying royalty payment shall not prevent Lender from at any time within three years after the Term of this Agreement questioning the validity or accuracy of such royalty statement or payment.
perturbation.changed_text: (vii) Receipt or acceptance by Lender (or its authorized representative) of a royalty statement or receipt or acceptance of any accompanying royalty payment shall not prevent Lender from at any time within one year after the Term of this Agreement questioning the validity or accuracy of such royalty statement or payment.
perturbation.explanation: The change reduces the time frame for questioning royalty statements from three years to one year, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law provides a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on a written contract. By reducing the contractual period to one year, the agreement attempts to shorten the time during which the lender can legally challenge the royalty statements, which is in conflict with California law where the contract says it will be governed.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the time frame for questioning royalty statements from three years to one year, conflicting with California Code of Civil Procedure Section 337, which establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on a written contract. The changed text therefore directly contradicts the established legal timeframe for such actions, undermining the lender's legal rights under California law. The snippet explicitly states: 'Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing...'

perturbation.original_text: MusclePharm will send all statements and payments, including all royalties, to the Lender to the address set forth in Section 20 below. MusclePharm will make all payments payable to the Lender.
perturbation.changed_text: MusclePharm will send all statements and payments, including all royalties, to the Lender to the address set forth in Section 20 below. MusclePharm will make all payments payable at the company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to allow for MusclePharm's discretion contradicts the requirement for timely and consistent wage payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 204 mandates that wages earned by an employee must be paid regularly, designating semi-monthly pay periods. Modifying the contract to allow royalty payments at the company's discretion introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with this legal obligation for timely and predictable compensation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 204. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 2. EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that p
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Labor Code Section 204(a), as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original contract specified that MusclePharm would make all payments payable to the Lender. The changed text, however, states that payments will be made 'at the company's discretion.' This introduces ambiguity regarding payment timing and regularity. Snippet 1 states that 'All wages... are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays.' The changed text undermines this legal requirement for regular and predictable wage payments, creating a direct conflict, as now payments are at the company's discretion, which is not compliant. This constitutes an 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the change violates state law related to time-sensitive obligations, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall be entitled to share such confidential information received by such party only with such party's representatives, legal and accounting advisors who shall agree to be bound by the confidentiality obligations set forth in this Section 26. During the term of this Agreement and for three (3) years thereafter, each party shall hold in strict confidence all such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall be entitled to share such confidential information received by such party only with such party's representatives, legal and accounting advisors who shall agree to be bound by the confidentiality obligations set forth in this Section 26. During the term of this Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter, each party shall hold in strict confidence all such information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification shortens the confidentiality period from three years to one year, which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, especially given the nature of the business and the potential for reverse engineering.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Reducing the contractual confidentiality period to one year may not adequately protect these trade secrets, potentially allowing for their disclosure and use after that period, contrary to the intent and protections offered by CUTSA. The three-year limit is more reflective of the time it would take a competitor to independently develop or reverse engineer the product in this business which is why the one-year limit could be an issue.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest is insufficient to protect trade secrets under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Snippet 1 references the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original contract's longer period aligns better with protecting trade secrets, whereas the shortened period could allow for premature disclosure, which directly undermines the purpose of trade secret law. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by weakening the protection of trade secrets as afforded under CUTSA.
